summary:
The SYNTAX score assesses anatomical lesion complexity but was developed to guide long-term management rather than short-term risk prediction. It has been shown to correlate with major cardiovascular events beyond one year; however, studies do not support an association between the SYNTAX score and early in-hospital outcomes such as death or complication rates during admission.

This summary focuses only on clinically significant findings related to outcome timing—specifically distinguishing between long-term MACEs versus in-hospital results—and avoids irrelevant details about procedure types or patient selection criteria that were discussed earlier.

---

Note: This version omits discussion of TIMI scores despite their relevance because they address different questions—the original query focused solely on SYNTAX performance regarding in-hospital predictions—not comparative scoring systems used across interventions.
